column1: === DiskANN vs Divergence: Cohere 100K, dim=768, k=100, single-thread ===
Hardware: EC2 i4i.2xlarge (Intel Xeon Platinum 8375C Ice Lake, 8 vCPU, NVMe SSD), 2026-03-09/10
Dataset: Cohere 100K, 768d, L2-normalized, cosine metric (squared_l2 ranking)
Ground truth: brute-force top-100 (same GT file, SHA256 verified)
DiskANN: Rust v0.48.0, Vamana disk-index, R=32, L_build=200
- PQ codes in DRAM: pq_compressed.bin 9.2MB + pq_pivots.bin 776KB ≈ 10 MB
- Vectors co-located in 4KB sectors on disk (1 sector read = adjacency + vector)
- beam_width=4, no cache (cache_hit% = 0.0% at all cache sizes tested)
Divergence FP32: NSW, m_max=32, ef_c=200, v3 BFS page-packed adjacency
- FP32 vectors in DRAM (VectorBank): 293 MB
- Adjacency on disk: 6.1 MB (1537 × 4KB pages, O_DIRECT)
- AdjacencyPool cache: 1 MB (perq-cold: pool.clear() before each query)
- Total DRAM: ~295 MB
Divergence SAQ v4: same graph, SAQ proxy (DRAM) + disk refine
- SAQ codes in DRAM: 87 MB (912 B/vec × 100K, rot+eqseg16, 12×64d segments, B=4)
- Refine vectors on disk: FP32 293 MB (3072 B/vec) or FP16 146.5 MB (1536 B/vec)
- Adjacency on disk: 6.1 MB (same v3 graph)
- AdjacencyPool cache: 1 MB (perq-cold)
- Total DRAM: ~89 MB (3.3× less than FP32, 9× more than DiskANN)
=== DiskANN Search Results (no cache, beam_width=4, 1 thread) ===
L   recall@100    QPS   mean_lat_us   p95_us   p999_us   IOs/q
=== Divergence FP32 Search Results (perq-cold, 1MB pool, W=4) ===
config           ef   recall@100    QPS   p50_ms   p99_ms   phy/q    hit%   mean_ms
NOTE: mean_ms = 1000/QPS (derived). Divergence reports p50 natively, not mean.
IO sizes: phy/q = 4KB adjacency page reads (O_DIRECT). Vectors are in DRAM (zero IO).
=== Divergence SAQ v4 Search Results (perq-cold, rot+eqseg16, W=4, SIMD) ===
Two-stage pipeline: SAQ proxy (DRAM) for graph traversal → FP32 disk refine.
SAQ dot product: AVX2+FMA SIMD kernel (dot_u8_f32_avx), 4.9× speedup at dim=64.
IO accounting: adj_phy/q = 4KB page reads; ref/q = 3072B vector reads.
Total io/q = adj_phy/q + ref/q (different read sizes — do NOT treat as equal).
config          ef   R   recall@100    QPS   p50_ms   p99_ms   adj/q   ref/q   io/q   ref_ms  dst_ms  mean_ms
SAQ-only (no refine, proxy recall):
NOTE: ref/q reads are 3072 bytes each (dim×4 = 768×4). adj/q reads are 4096 bytes each.
Total bytes read/q at R=160: 135.9×4096 + 160×3072 = 557K adj + 491K vec = 1.05 MB/q.
=== Hub Pinning Results (2026-03-10, perq-cold, SAQ+R160) ===
Hub pinning: pin top BFS-ordered adjacency pages into AdjacencyPool before search.
Pinned pages survive pool.clear() between queries — they stay cached across all queries.
BFS-ordered pages 0..N are a proxy for hub pages (entry nodes visited first in BFS).
config           ef   R   pin  recall@100    QPS   p50_ms   p99_ms   adj/q  phy/q   ref/q   io/q   ref_ms  dst_ms  mean_ms  hit%   mis/q
Hub pinning impact:
io_ms: 5.93→5.43ms (-8.4%) — fewer cold adjacency page reads
Recall unchanged (0.963) — pinning only affects cache hits, not search quality
pin128 requested 128 pages, 120 actually pinned (pool capacity limited some sets)
=== Zero-Alloc Refine (2026-03-10) ===
VectorReader::read_cosine_distance() computes cosine distance directly on the
aligned IO buffer — eliminates one Vec<f32> heap allocation + memcpy per refine
candidate (160 allocs per query at R=160).
Comparison to pre-optimization (2026-03-09) baseline:
ref_ms: 1.85→1.81ms (SAQ+R160, no pin) — modest 2.2% improvement
The optimization saves CPU allocation overhead (~160×768×4B = 480KB memcpy/q).
At this scale, IO dominates refine latency; benefit grows at higher R or on faster IO.
=== Int8 Refine Experiment (2026-03-10) ===
Hypothesis: int8 vectors on disk (768 B/vec) instead of FP32 (3072 B/vec) reduce
refine IO by 3× and improve QPS. Encoding: round(f32 * 127), clamped to [-127, 127].
IO byte accounting (validated):
FP32: VectorReader submits read_at with AlignedBuf::new(total_read).
AlignedBuf rounds size to 512B (IO_SIZE_ALIGNMENT=512, NOT 4096).
For dim=768: vec_bytes=3072, total_read=3072 (already 512-aligned).
Measured via read_at return n: 3072 bytes per refine read.
Int8: vec_bytes=768, total_read=round_up(768+intra, 512)=1024.
Measured via perf.refine_bytes (sum of total_read per read_at): 160 KB/q.
R=160: 160 × 1024 = 160 KB/q refine IO (3× reduction, confirmed).
--- Performance: POSITIVE ---
After removing unnecessary /127 scaling (cos(q, c/127) = dot(q,c)/(||q||·||c||),
the 1/127 cancels in the ratio), int8 refine is faster and reads fewer bytes:
config           ef   R   recall@100    QPS   p50_ms   ref_ms   refine_bytes/q
ref_ms: int8 1.64ms vs FP32 1.76ms — int8 is 7% faster (fewer IO bytes).
Refine bytes: 3× reduction (160 KB vs 480 KB), measured not estimated.
--- Quality: NEGATIVE — int8 is NOT a valid final refine stage ---
Recall drops 3.4% (0.963→0.929) and saturates: R=200 still only 0.928.
Int8 quantization error corrupts the final re-ranking. At dim=768, typical component
magnitude ~1/√768 ≈ 0.036, int8 resolution 1/127 ≈ 0.008 — only ~4.6 levels per
component. The refine stage IS the correction stage; quantization error here has
no further correction. Increasing R cannot fix systematic ranking errors.
Int8+pin128 at 97.7 QPS is the fastest config ONLY if you accept 0.929 recall.
At iso-recall 0.963, it is NOT a valid replacement for FP32 refine.
--- Viable path: three-stage pipeline ---
Use int8 as a filter stage (Stage 2), not the final stage:
Stage 1: SAQ proxy (DRAM) → top-R candidates (R=160)
Stage 2: Int8 refine (disk, 1KB/vec) → top-40 candidates (cheap filter)
Stage 3: FP32 refine (disk, 3KB/vec) → top-k exact re-ranking (40 reads)
Expected IO: 160×1024 + 40×3072 = 160KB + 120KB = 280 KB/q (vs 480 KB FP32-only).
Recall should match FP32 refine (Stage 3 is exact).
=== FP16 Refine Experiment (2026-03-10) ===
Hypothesis: FP16 vectors on disk (1536 B/vec) instead of FP32 (3072 B/vec) reduce
refine IO by 2× while preserving near-lossless recall. IEEE 754 half-precision has
~3 decimal digits of precision vs int8's ~2.1 digits.
Implementation:
Writer: write_vectors_fp16_file() in crates/storage/src/vectors.rs
- f32→f16 conversion: manual IEEE 754 bit manipulation (handles normal, subnormal, inf, NaN)
- 512B EOF padding for O_DIRECT friendliness
Reader: Fp16VectorReader in crates/engine/src/io.rs
- read_cosine_distance() returns (f32, usize) — distance + actual IO bytes
- Cosine distance computed on-the-fly from IO buffer (zero heap alloc)
SIMD: cosine_dot_fp16() dispatches to F16C+AVX2+FMA kernel at runtime
- VCVTPH2PS: converts 8 × f16 → 8 × f32 in one instruction
- FMA: dot += q*v, norm += v*v fused in same loop
- Scalar fallback for non-F16C CPUs
Search: disk_graph_search_pipe_v3_refine_fp16() in crates/engine/src/search.rs
- Same structure as FP32 refine but uses Fp16VectorReader
IO byte accounting (validated):
FP16: vec_bytes=1536 (768×2), total_read=round_up(1536+intra, 512)=1536.
Measured via perf.refine_bytes: 240 KB/q at R=160 (1536×160 = 240KB, minor rounding).
R=160: 240 KB/q refine IO (2× reduction vs FP32's 480 KB/q, confirmed).
--- Scalar vs SIMD: F16C is critical ---
Without F16C SIMD, naive f16→f32 conversion loop with branching per element is 1.8× SLOWER
than FP32 in compute time, completely negating the 2× IO savings:
config                ref_ms (scalar)   ref_ms (F16C SIMD)   ref_ms (FP32)
The f16→f32 conversion has complex branching (subnormal/normal/inf) per element.
VCVTPH2PS does this in hardware for 8 elements in one cycle.
LESSON: never ship FP16 without F16C SIMD — scalar conversion is a performance trap.
--- Quality: POSITIVE — FP16 matches FP32 recall exactly ---
config           ef   R   recall@100    QPS   p50_ms   ref_ms   refine_bytes/q
Recall is IDENTICAL at every R point. FP16 has enough precision (~3 decimal digits)
to perfectly preserve cosine distance ranking for L2-normalized vectors in [-1, 1].
This is the key advantage over int8 (which drops recall by 3.4%).
--- FP16 + Hub Pinning (best overall config) ---
config            ef   R   pin  recall@100    QPS   p50_ms   ref_ms   refine_bytes/q
FP16+pin128 is the new best iso-recall config: 97.4 QPS, p50=9.1ms, 240 KB refine IO/q.
vs FP32+pin128: same recall, +0.4% QPS, -50% refine IO bytes
vs Int8+pin128: +3.4% better recall (0.963 vs 0.929), -1% QPS (97.4 vs 98.4)
=== Three-Stage Pipeline Experiment (2026-03-10) — NEGATIVE RESULT ===
Hypothesis: SAQ → int8 filter (cheap) → FP32 exact (expensive) uses int8 as a
pre-filter to reduce the number of FP32 refine reads.
Implementation: disk_graph_search_pipe_v3_refine_3stage() in search.rs
Stage 1: SAQ proxy graph traversal (DRAM) → top-R candidates (R=160)
Stage 2: Int8 filter (disk, 1KB/vec) → all R candidates rescored, keep top-T
Stage 3: FP32 exact refine (disk, 3KB/vec) → top-k from T candidates
Results (perq-cold, ef=200, R=160):
config            T    recall   p50_ms    QPS   ref_ms   refine_bytes/q   refines/q
Why it fails:
1. To beat FP32 baseline refine bytes: 160×1KB + T×3KB < 480KB ⇒ T < 107.
2. Iso-recall requires T≥120 (0.962) → 520 KB > 480 KB → WORSE than just FP32.
3. T≤80 destroys recall because int8 ranking quality can't safely prune.
4. The int8 stage adds latency (160 extra reads) without saving enough FP32 reads.
CONCLUSION: Int8 as filter is dead unless the intermediate representation becomes
much more rank-faithful. Do not iterate further on int8 filter.
=== Iso-Recall Comparison: recall ≈ 96.3% (Updated 2026-03-10) ===
Latency: DiskANN reports mean, Divergence reports p50. QPS is comparable.
recall   QPS    lat_ms*   IO/q     DRAM     IO_bytes/q
★ = NEW BEST iso-recall config
* m = mean latency, p = p50 (median)
At iso-recall ~96.3%:
QPS:  FP32-DRAM (109.1) vs DiskANN (48.1) = 2.27×
QPS:  SAQ+FP16+pin128 (97.4) vs DiskANN (48.1) = 2.02×
QPS:  SAQ+FP32+pin128 (97.0) vs DiskANN (48.1) = 2.02×
IO bytes: FP32-DRAM (554 KB) vs DiskANN (2.07 MB) = 3.74× fewer bytes
IO bytes: SAQ+FP16+pin128 (727 KB) vs DiskANN (2.07 MB) = 2.85× fewer bytes
IO bytes: SAQ+FP32+pin128 (974 KB) vs DiskANN (2.07 MB) = 2.13× fewer bytes
DRAM: DiskANN (10 MB) vs SAQ (89 MB) vs FP32 (295 MB)
=== Three-Way DRAM–QPS–IO Tradeoff (Updated 2026-03-10) ===
The five configs represent points on a DRAM vs IO vs QPS surface:
System                       DRAM    QPS@96.3%   IO bytes/q   Design choice
DiskANN                       10 MB    48.1        2.07 MB    Vectors on disk, PQ proxy
Divergence SAQ+FP16+pin128 ★  89 MB    97.4         727 KB    SAQ DRAM, FP16 disk, F16C SIMD
Divergence SAQ+FP32+pin128    89 MB    97.0         974 KB    SAQ DRAM, FP32 disk, SIMD
Divergence SAQ+FP16           89 MB    90.5         797 KB    SAQ DRAM, FP16 disk (no pin)
Divergence SAQ+FP32           89 MB    88.9        1.05 MB    SAQ DRAM, FP32 disk (no pin)
Divergence FP32              295 MB   109.1         554 KB    Full vectors in DRAM
★ = NEW BEST iso-recall config (among disk-refine configs)
Each step from DiskANN → SAQ → FP32 trades DRAM for QPS:
DiskANN → SAQ+FP16+pin128:  +79 MB DRAM → +103% QPS, -65% IO bytes
SAQ+FP16+pin128 → FP32:    +206 MB DRAM → +12% QPS, -24% IO bytes
FP16 vs FP32 refine (same DRAM):
FP16 saves 25% total IO bytes (727 vs 974 KB/q) with same recall and +0.4% QPS.
FP16 advantage grows at larger N: fewer bytes = less NVMe contention under multi-core.
At 100K scale (295 MB), all configs are practical.
At 10M scale: FP32 needs 29.3 GB, SAQ needs 8.7 GB, DiskANN needs ~100 MB.
At 100M scale: FP32 needs 293 GB (impractical), SAQ needs 87 GB, DiskANN needs ~1 GB.
=== Analysis ===
1. FP16 REFINE IS THE NEW DEFAULT (REPLACES FP32):
FP16 matches FP32 recall exactly at every R point (0.954/0.963/0.964).
With F16C SIMD, FP16 ref_ms is 6% FASTER than FP32 (1.67 vs 1.77ms).
IO bytes are 2× smaller (240 vs 480 KB/q at R=160).
Combined with pin128: p50=9.1ms, QPS=97.4 — the new best iso-recall config.
CRITICAL: F16C SIMD is mandatory. Without it, scalar f16→f32 is 1.8× SLOWER than FP32.
All modern x86 CPUs (Ivy Bridge+, 2012+) support F16C, including all EC2 instances.
2. INT8 REFINE AND 3-STAGE ARE DEAD ENDS:
Int8 recall ceiling is 0.929 (vs 0.963 FP32) — only ~4.6 quantization levels per
component at dim=768. Cannot be fixed by increasing R (saturates at 0.928).
Three-stage (int8 filter → FP32 exact) requires T≥120 for iso-recall but then
total IO (520 KB) EXCEEDS FP32-only (480 KB). Do not iterate further on int8.
3. SAQ WITH SIMD CLOSES THE QPS GAP:
SAQ uses 3.3× less DRAM than FP32 (89 vs 295 MB) while retaining 89% of QPS
(97.4 vs 109.1 with FP16+pin128). SIMD cut SAQ compute from 3.6ms to 1.8ms (-50%),
bringing SAQ+FP16+pin128 p50 to 9.1ms. SAQ proxy-only (warm) is now FASTER than
FP32 (7.1ms vs 7.2ms p50) — SAQ compute overhead fully hidden.
4. HUB PINNING PROVIDES MODEST BUT FREE IMPROVEMENT:
Pin128 cuts phy/q by 13.4% (136→118 page reads), QPS +7.6% (90.5→97.4 with FP16).
The improvement is "free" — no DRAM increase (pinned pages are within the existing
1MB pool), no recall change, no code complexity. Most value comes from pinning
the first 64 pages; pin128 gives diminishing returns vs pin64.
5. IO ACCOUNTING IS HONEST:
SAQ+FP16 io/q = 295.8 (136 adj pages + 160 vec reads).
Adj reads are 4KB, FP16 vec reads are 1.5KB, so total bytes = 797 KB/q.
With pin128: 118 adj + 160 FP16 vec = 727 KB/q.
DiskANN io/q = 506.1 (all 4KB sector reads) = 2.07 MB/q.
SAQ+FP16+pin128 reads 2.85× fewer bytes per query.
6. GRAPH QUALITY GAP PERSISTS:
DiskANN needs L=500 (506 IOs) to reach recall 96.8%.
Divergence reaches 96.3% at ef=200 (136 page reads).
Vamana disk-index construction quality is lower for high-dim cosine.
7. DiskANN CACHE REMAINS INEFFECTIVE:
cache_hit% = 0.0% at all sizes. This is a DiskANN Rust implementation issue
(cache only stores BFS-ordered nodes at init, doesn't cache search reads).
8. LATENCY METRIC MISMATCH:
DiskANN mean vs Divergence p50. QPS (= nq/wall_time) is directly comparable.
DiskANN mean ≈ p50 for this workload (symmetric latency distribution).
=== Disk & DRAM Usage ===
Component                    DiskANN         Divg FP32       Divg SAQ+FP32   Divg SAQ+FP16
Vectors (disk)               in 4KB sectors  293 MB          293 MB          146.5 MB
Adjacency (disk)             in 4KB sectors  6.1 MB          6.1 MB          6.1 MB
disk.index (combined)        391 MB          -               -               -
Total on disk                ~402 MB         ~300 MB         ~300 MB         ~153 MB
PQ codes (DRAM)              9.2 MB          -               -               -
PQ pivots (DRAM)             776 KB          -               -               -
FP32 vectors (DRAM)          -               293 MB          -               -
SAQ codes (DRAM)             -               -               87 MB           87 MB
adj_index (DRAM)             -               782 KB          782 KB          782 KB
AdjacencyPool cache (DRAM)   -               1 MB            1 MB            1 MB
Total DRAM                   ~10 MB          ~295 MB         ~89 MB          ~89 MB
=== Test Methodology (2026-03-10) ===
Hardware:
- EC2 i4i.2xlarge: Intel Xeon Platinum 8375C (Ice Lake) @ 2.90GHz, 8 vCPU
- 1.7TB NVMe instance SSD (local, not EBS), XFS filesystem
- Linux 6.8.0-1047-aws, io_uring for all IO
Dataset:
- Cohere 100K: 100,000 vectors, 768 dimensions, L2-normalized (cosine metric)
- Source: Cohere embedding model output, pre-normalized to unit length
- Queries: 1000 vectors from same distribution; first 100 used for benchmarks
- Ground truth: brute-force top-100 (exhaustive scan), verified by SHA256
Index construction:
- NSW graph: m_max=32, ef_construction=200 (same for all Divergence configs)
- BFS reorder: entry-node BFS assigns consecutive page IDs to neighbor clusters
- v3 page-packed adjacency: variable-degree neighbors packed into 4KB pages
- O_DIRECT for all disk IO (bypasses OS page cache, honest NVMe latency)
SAQ encoding:
- Tool: scripts/saq_eval.cpp linking SAQ C++ library (SIGMOD '26 reference impl)
- Config: avg_bits=4.0, rotation=random, eqseg=16 (12 × 64d segments)
- Each segment: 64-dim, 4-bit codes, 64×64 random rotation matrix
- Export: saq_unpacked_eq16.bin (912 B/vec: 768 u8 codes + 12 × 3 f32 factors)
- Loaded into DRAM as SaqVectorBank for proxy distance computation
Benchmark execution:
- Single-thread, single io_uring ring (monoio runtime)
- AdjacencyPool: 256 pages = 1 MB (5% of 1537 v3 pages)
- Prefetch: W=4 (issue 4 adjacency page reads ahead of current beam)
- Per-query cold: pool.clear() before each query (worst-case, no cross-query caching)
- Exception: hub-pinned pages survive clear()
- 100 queries, no warmup for cold runs (10 warmup queries for warm runs)
- Refine: monoio::spawn + read_cosine_distance (zero-alloc since 2026-03-10)
- Refine inflight: 32 concurrent vector reads per batch
- FP16 refine: Fp16VectorReader + cosine_dot_fp16 F16C+AVX2+FMA SIMD kernel
- VCVTPH2PS: 8 × f16 → f32 per cycle, fused with FMA dot+norm
- Runtime detection via is_x86_feature_detected! (scalar fallback available)
- vectors_fp16.dat generated per-run by write_vectors_fp16_file()
- Int8 refine (deprecated): Int8VectorReader, raw code cosine (1/127 cancels)
- Three-stage (deprecated): int8 filter → FP32 exact, disk_graph_search_pipe_v3_refine_3stage
Hub pinning procedure:
- pin_pages(&[0..N]) pins first N BFS-ordered pages into AdjacencyPool
- Pinned entries are marked in the set-associative cache, immune to eviction + clear()
- BFS order ≈ hub pages (entry nodes are high-degree hubs, visited first)
- MAX_PINNED_PER_SET=4 limits pins per set — pin128 achieves 120 actual pins
DiskANN configuration:
- DiskANN Rust v0.48.0, Vamana disk-index, R=32, L_build=200
- beam_width=4, no effective cache (cache_hit% = 0.0% at all tested sizes)
- Same dataset (exported to DiskANN .bin format), same ground truth file
- Run: 2026-03-09 on same EC2 instance
Fairness rules:
- Same hardware, same dataset, same ground truth file
- Both use O_DIRECT (no OS page cache advantage)
- QPS = nq/wall_time (directly comparable across systems)
- Latency: DiskANN reports mean, Divergence reports p50 (noted in all tables)
- IO accounting: byte-level (4KB adj vs 3KB vec reads counted separately)
- DRAM: all in-memory data structures counted (PQ/SAQ codes, vectors, index, cache)
=== Optimization History ===
Date        Change                                  Impact (SAQ+R160 perq-cold)
2026-03-09  Baseline (scalar SAQ dot product)        p50=11.8ms, QPS=77.6
2026-03-09  AVX2+FMA SIMD kernel (dot_u8_f32_avx)   p50=9.8ms (-17%), QPS=90.7 (+17%)
2026-03-10  Zero-alloc refine (read_cosine_distance) ref_ms 1.85→1.81ms (-2.2%)
2026-03-10  Int8 refine                              REJECTED — recall drops 3.4%, int8 too lossy for dim=768
2026-03-10  Three-stage (int8 filter → FP32)         REJECTED — can't beat FP32-only IO at iso-recall
2026-03-10  FP16 refine (scalar)                     ref_ms 1.77→3.23 (1.8× SLOWER, compute-bound)
2026-03-10  FP16 + F16C SIMD (VCVTPH2PS)            ref_ms 3.23→1.67 (5.3% faster than FP32), IO -50%
2026-03-10  FP16+pin128 (★ NEW BEST)                p50=9.1ms, QPS=97.4, 240KB refine IO/q
Combined    All optimizations vs baseline            p50 11.8→9.1ms (-23%), QPS 77.6→97.4 (+26%)
=== Remaining TODO ===
1. [x] AVX2+FMA SIMD kernel for SAQ dot product: 4.9-9.3× kernel speedup, 17% e2e
2. [ ] SAQ ef sweep: run SAQ+R160 at ef=225,250,300 for iso-recall 96.8%+
3. [x] Hub pinning (pin64/128): +4.4% QPS, -13% adj IO (perq-cold)
4. [x] Zero-alloc refine: -2.2% ref_ms (read_cosine_distance)
5. [x] Int8 refine: REJECTED — recall drops 3.4% (quantization too lossy for dim=768)
6. [x] Three-stage (int8 filter): REJECTED — can't beat FP32-only IO at iso-recall
7. [x] FP16 refine + F16C SIMD: ★ NEW BEST — iso-recall, -50% refine IO, +26% QPS vs baseline
8. [ ] Adaptive refine stopping: per-query early stop when top-k stabilizes (reduce avg R)
9. [ ] Vector page packing: BFS reorder + pack vectors into pages + VectorPool cache
(PageANN-style: top-R candidates share vector pages, cache pages not individuals)
10. [ ] Improve SAQ proxy recall to lower required R (better segmentation / more bits)
11. [ ] DiskANN R=64 or L_build=300: test if graph quality gap narrows
12. [ ] Multi-thread comparison
13. [ ] SAQ packed multi-segment: reduce 912 B/vec → ~528 B/vec (5.8× vs FP32)
14. [ ] Divergence mean latency: add mean_ms to BenchResult for fair latency comparison
=== Code Map for Future Agents ===
Key source files:
crates/storage/src/vectors.rs    — write_vectors_file (FP32), write_vectors_fp16_file, write_vectors_int8_file
crates/engine/src/io.rs          — VectorReader (FP32), Fp16VectorReader, Int8VectorReader
cosine_dot_fp16() with F16C+AVX2+FMA SIMD kernel
crates/engine/src/search.rs      — disk_graph_search_pipe_v3_refine (FP32 refine)
disk_graph_search_pipe_v3_refine_fp16 (FP16 refine)
disk_graph_search_pipe_v3_refine_int8 (int8 refine, deprecated)
disk_graph_search_pipe_v3_refine_3stage (3-stage, deprecated)
crates/engine/src/lib.rs         — public exports
crates/engine/tests/disk_search.rs — exp_saq_graph test (all benchmarks)
run_bench_v3_refine_fp16(), run_bench_v3_refine_int8()
crates/engine/src/perf.rs        — SearchPerfContext (refine_bytes, refine_count, refine_ns)
results/diskann_vs_divergence_2026-03-09.tsv — this file
Benchmark execution pattern:
# Local: compile check
cargo check -p divergence-engine --tests
# Deploy to EC2 (preserves SAQ data):
./scripts/deploy_ec2.sh <EC2_IP>
# EC2: build + run
source ~/.cargo/env && cd /mnt/nvme/divergence
cargo build --release -p divergence-engine --tests
BENCH_DIR=/mnt/nvme/bench COHERE_DIR=/mnt/nvme/divergence/data/cohere_100k \
COHERE_N=100000 SAQ_SUFFIX=_eq16 \
cargo test --release -p divergence-engine --test disk_search exp_saq_graph \
-- --nocapture --ignored
Required env vars:
BENCH_DIR — directory for O_DIRECT index files (must be on NVMe, not tmpfs)
COHERE_DIR — path to cohere_100k dataset (vectors + queries + SAQ data)
COHERE_N — number of vectors (100000)
SAQ_SUFFIX — SAQ data suffix (_eq16 for rot+eqseg16)
Design principles:
- All refine readers compute cosine distance IN-PLACE on the IO buffer (zero alloc)
- All readers return actual IO bytes submitted for honest accounting
- F16C SIMD is runtime-detected (is_x86_feature_detected!), falls back to scalar
- Each search function is a pure async fn: query → Vec<ScoredId>
- Perf counters are per-query (caller manages lifecycle via SearchPerfContext)
